topic: Language - Negative Impls and Auto Traits

turns.content: Imagine I have a struct called DataHolder that wraps an Rc<T> but also contains a RefCell. I want to make it !Send because the RefCell part makes it non-send, yet I need to pass the whole thing around in contexts where Send is expected. I'm stuck on how negative impls actually interact with auto traits and whether my code will ever compile if I just write impl !Send for DataHolder.

The issue here is that negative trait bounds don't work like you might think for auto traits such as Send or Sync. When you write impl !Send for MyType, the Rust compiler checks every type T where MyType implements Trait<T> and ensures it doesn't conflict with the negative bound. For DataHolder containing a RefCell, the real problem is that RefCell itself violates Send, so any container wrapping it will also be non-send by default unless you explicitly opt out using PhantomData. The key insight is that negative impls only affect types that implement Trait<T> for some T; they do not force all types to respect !Send automatically without considering the actual dependencies in your struct.

So if I just write impl !Sync and impl !Send for DataHolder, will that actually prevent the compiler from checking whether DataHolder contains something Send? It feels like I'm just adding noise to my code when I really need to fix the underlying type issue. Is there a simpler way to handle this without negative trait bounds?

Negative impls are essential for soundness because they allow you to express that certain types cannot be shared across threads or synchronized, but they don't magically make your entire struct compliant with Send or Sync if it contains incompatible types. The real solution when wrapping a RefCell is using PhantomData<T> as a marker type in your struct instead of actually storing the problematic value directly. By declaring impl !Send for DataHolder and then adding a PhantomData<RefCell> field that you don't actually use, you can signal to the compiler that this specific type should never be Send or Sync without breaking other parts of your codebase.

That sounds promising but I'm worried about accidentally using PhantomData in production. How exactly do I combine negative impls with auto trait propagation rules so that my DataHolder remains non-send even if someone tries to use it in a context expecting Send? Is there a way to make the compiler stop complaining when I pass DataHolder to a function that requires Send?

When you define impl !Send for DataHolder and include PhantomData<RefCell> in your struct, the auto trait propagation rules ensure that any type T where DataHolder implements Trait<T> will inherit this non-send status. The compiler won't allow you to pass DataHolder to a function requiring Send because the negative bound effectively blocks the automatic inference of Send for your type. If you need to work with such types in multi-threaded code, you must explicitly opt out using PhantomData and avoid passing these types across thread boundaries unless you've carefully analyzed their internal composition.

I still think I'm misunderstanding something fundamental here. Can you clarify how negative impls interact with the auto traits like Send so that when I write impl !Send for MyType, it actually prevents any type T from being considered Send if MyType implements Trait<T>? It seems too restrictive and I fear my code will just break everywhere.

Negative trait bounds are sound because they constrain how types can be composed in the type system. When you write impl !Send for DataHolder, you're telling the compiler that this specific type cannot participate in Send-related operations regardless of its internal fields. The auto traits like Send and Sync propagate through trait implementations but negative bounds override this propagation by explicitly stating that certain combinations are forbidden. Your code won't break everywhere; instead, it will correctly reject any attempt to use DataHolder in a Send context while allowing you to safely use it in single-threaded scenarios or with PhantomData markers.

Okay I think I get the basic idea but I'm still confused about how this affects my actual Rust code. If I have a function that takes an argument of type MyType and requires that argument to be Send, will negative impls prevent me from calling this function? And what happens if I want to use PhantomData instead of actually storing the problematic value?

Yes, negative impls will prevent you from passing DataHolder to a function requiring Send because the compiler checks every type that implements Trait<T> against your !Send declaration. If you replace the actual RefCell with PhantomData<RefCell>, the struct becomes smaller and safer for single-threaded use cases while still maintaining the !Send guarantee through auto trait propagation. The key is understanding that PhantomData acts as a lightweight marker that preserves the negative bound without adding runtime overhead, allowing you to express intent clearly without breaking your type system.
